cards.front: What is an atom?
cards.back: The smallest part of an element that can exist.
cards.explanation: All substances are made of atoms, and an atom is the smallest part of an element that can exist.

choices.text: The smallest part of an element that can exist.
choices.feedback: Correct: this is the specification definition of an atom.

choices.text: The smallest part of a compound that can exist.
choices.feedback: An atom is defined as part of an element, not a compound.

choices.text: A group of particles that are not chemically combined.
choices.feedback: This does not describe the smallest part of an element.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 67-75; 5.1.1.1 Atoms, elements and compounds
sources.excerpt:
All substances are made of atoms. An atom is the smallest part of
an element that can exist.

cards.front: What are isotopes of an element?
cards.back: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.
cards.explanation: Isotopes belong to the same element but have different numbers of neutrons.

choices.text: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.
choices.feedback: Correct: the element is the same and the neutron number differs.

choices.text: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of protons.
choices.feedback: The stated difference between isotopes is the number of neutrons, not protons.

choices.text: Atoms of different elements with the same number of neutrons.
choices.feedback: Isotopes are atoms of the same element.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 67-75; 5.1.1.5 Size and mass of atoms
sources.excerpt:
Atoms of the same element can have different numbers of
neutrons; these atoms are called isotopes of that element.

cards.front: State the Group 7 reactivity trend and the rule for halogen displacement from an aqueous salt solution.
cards.back: Reactivity decreases down Group 7; a more reactive halogen can displace a less reactive one from an aqueous salt solution.
cards.explanation: Group 7 becomes less reactive down the group, and a more reactive halogen can displace a less reactive halogen from an aqueous solution of its salt.

choices.text: Reactivity decreases down Group 7; a more reactive halogen can displace a less reactive one from an aqueous salt solution.
choices.feedback: Correct: this gives both the trend and displacement rule.

choices.text: Reactivity increases down Group 7; a less reactive halogen displaces a more reactive one.
choices.feedback: Both the reactivity trend and the displacement direction are reversed.

choices.text: Reactivity is constant down Group 7; halogens cannot displace one another.
choices.feedback: Reactivity changes down the group, and a more reactive halogen can cause displacement.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 67-75; 5.1.2.6 Group 7
sources.excerpt:
In Group 7, the reactivity of the elements decreases going down the
group.
A more reactive halogen can displace a less reactive halogen from
an aqueous solution of its salt.

cards.front: What particles or electron arrangements characterise ionic, covalent and metallic bonding?
cards.back: Ionic: oppositely charged ions; covalent: atoms sharing electron pairs; metallic: atoms sharing delocalised electrons.
cards.explanation: Ionic bonding involves oppositely charged ions, covalent bonding involves atoms sharing pairs of electrons, and metallic bonding involves atoms sharing delocalised electrons.

choices.text: Ionic: oppositely charged ions; covalent: atoms sharing electron pairs; metallic: atoms sharing delocalised electrons.
choices.feedback: Correct: each bonding type is matched to its specified particles or electrons.

choices.text: Ionic: atoms sharing electron pairs; covalent: oppositely charged ions; metallic: atoms sharing delocalised electrons.
choices.feedback: This swaps the descriptions of ionic and covalent bonding.

choices.text: Ionic: oppositely charged ions; covalent: atoms sharing delocalised electrons; metallic: atoms sharing electron pairs.
choices.feedback: This swaps the descriptions of covalent and metallic bonding.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 75-84; 5.2.1.1 Chemical bonds
sources.excerpt:
There are three types of strong chemical bonds: ionic, covalent and
metallic. For ionic bonding the particles are oppositely charged ions.
For covalent bonding the particles are atoms which share pairs of
electrons. For metallic bonding the particles are atoms which share
delocalised electrons.

cards.front: When do ionic compounds conduct electricity, and why?
cards.back: When melted or dissolved in water, because their ions are free to move and carry charge.
cards.explanation: Melting or dissolving an ionic compound in water allows its ions to move, so charge can flow.

choices.text: When melted or dissolved in water, because their ions are free to move and carry charge.
choices.feedback: Correct: mobile ions allow charge to flow.

choices.text: When solid, because their ions are free to move and carry charge.
choices.feedback: The conducting conditions stated are melted or dissolved in water.

choices.text: When melted or dissolved in water, because neutral molecules carry charge.
choices.feedback: The mobile charge carriers identified are ions, not neutral molecules.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 75-84; 5.2.2.3 Properties of ionic compounds
sources.excerpt:
When melted or dissolved in water, ionic compounds conduct
electricity because the ions are free to move and so charge can
flow.

cards.front: What is overcome when a small molecular substance melts or boils?
cards.back: Weak intermolecular forces are overcome; the covalent bonds are not.
cards.explanation: Small molecular substances have weak forces between molecules. Melting or boiling overcomes these intermolecular forces rather than the covalent bonds.

choices.text: Weak intermolecular forces are overcome; the covalent bonds are not.
choices.feedback: Correct: state changes overcome forces between molecules.

choices.text: The strong covalent bonds within each molecule are overcome.
choices.feedback: The covalent bonds are not the forces overcome during melting or boiling.

choices.text: Both intermolecular forces and covalent bonds are overcome.
choices.feedback: Only the intermolecular forces are identified as being overcome.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 75-84; 5.2.2.4 Properties of small molecules
sources.excerpt:
These substances have only weak forces between the molecules
(intermolecular forces). It is these intermolecular forces that are
overcome, not the covalent bonds, when the substance melts or
boils.

cards.front: Describe the bonding and electron arrangement in graphite.
cards.back: Each carbon forms three covalent bonds in hexagonal layers; there are no covalent bonds between layers, and one electron per carbon is delocalised.
cards.explanation: Graphite consists of hexagonal layers in which each carbon forms three covalent bonds. There are no covalent bonds between layers, and one electron from each carbon is delocalised.

choices.text: Each carbon forms three covalent bonds in hexagonal layers; there are no covalent bonds between layers, and one electron per carbon is delocalised.
choices.feedback: Correct: this includes graphite's layers, bonds and delocalised electron.

choices.text: Each carbon forms four covalent bonds in one rigid network, with no delocalised electrons.
choices.feedback: Graphite has three covalent bonds per carbon, layers and a delocalised electron.

choices.text: Each carbon forms two covalent bonds in chains, and all electrons are localised.
choices.feedback: The specified structure has three bonds per carbon in hexagonal layers and delocalised electrons.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 75-84; 5.2.3.2 Graphite
sources.excerpt:
In graphite, each carbon atom forms three covalent bonds with
three other carbon atoms, forming layers of hexagonal rings which
have no covalent bonds between the layers.
In graphite, one electron from each carbon atom is delocalised.

cards.front: What does the law of conservation of mass state for a chemical reaction?
cards.back: No atoms are lost or made, so the mass of the products equals the mass of the reactants.
cards.explanation: Because a chemical reaction neither loses nor makes atoms, the total product mass equals the total reactant mass.

choices.text: No atoms are lost or made, so the mass of the products equals the mass of the reactants.
choices.feedback: Correct: atom conservation gives equal total masses.

choices.text: Some atoms are destroyed, so the products always have less mass.
choices.feedback: The law states that no atoms are lost.

choices.text: New atoms are made, so the products always have more mass.
choices.feedback: The law states that no atoms are made.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 84-88; 5.3.1.1 Conservation of mass and balanced chemical equations
sources.excerpt:
The law of conservation of mass states that no atoms are lost or
made during a chemical reaction so the mass of the products
equals the mass of the reactants.

cards.front: How is the relative formula mass, Mr, of a compound found?
cards.back: Add the relative atomic masses of all the atoms in the numbers shown in the formula.
cards.explanation: Relative formula mass is the sum of the relative atomic masses, accounting for the numbers of atoms shown in the formula.

choices.text: Add the relative atomic masses of all the atoms in the numbers shown in the formula.
choices.feedback: Correct: Mr is a sum using the atom numbers in the formula.

choices.text: Find the average of the relative atomic masses shown in the formula.
choices.feedback: Relative formula mass is a sum, not an average.

choices.text: Use only the relative atomic mass of the heaviest atom.
choices.feedback: All atoms in the numbers shown must be included.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 84-88; 5.3.1.2 Relative formula mass
sources.excerpt:
The relative formula mass (Mr) of a compound is the sum of the
relative atomic masses of the atoms in the numbers shown in the
formula.

cards.front: Why may a reaction appear to change mass when a reactant or product is a gas?
cards.back: Usually, a gaseous reactant or product has not had its mass taken into account.
cards.explanation: An apparent mass change can usually be explained by the mass of a gaseous reactant or product not being included.

choices.text: Usually, a gaseous reactant or product has not had its mass taken into account.
choices.feedback: Correct: the apparent change comes from unaccounted gas mass.

choices.text: Atoms have been destroyed, so the total mass has fallen.
choices.feedback: The stated explanation is unaccounted gas mass, not destroyed atoms.

choices.text: Atoms have been created, so the total mass has risen.
choices.feedback: The stated explanation is unaccounted gas mass, not newly created atoms.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 84-88; 5.3.1.3 Mass changes when a reactant or product is a gas
sources.excerpt:
Some reactions may appear to involve a change in mass but this
can usually be explained because a reactant or product is a gas
and its mass has not been taken into account.

cards.front: How can the concentration of a solution be measured in terms of mass and volume?
cards.back: Mass per given volume of solution, for example grams per dm3 (g/dm3).
cards.explanation: A solution's concentration can be expressed as mass per given volume, with g/dm3 given as an example.

choices.text: Mass per given volume of solution, for example grams per dm3 (g/dm3).
choices.feedback: Correct: concentration can be measured as mass per volume.

choices.text: Volume of solution per given mass, for example dm3/g.
choices.feedback: This reverses the specified mass-per-volume relationship.

choices.text: Mass of solvent per given volume of solution.
choices.feedback: The measure is mass per volume of solution, not specifically the solvent mass.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 84-88; 5.3.2.5 Concentration of solutions
sources.excerpt:
Many chemical reactions take place in solutions. The concentration
of a solution can be measured in mass per given volume of
solution, eg grams per dm3 (g/dm3).

cards.front: What is the reactivity rule for one metal displacing another from a compound?
cards.back: A more reactive metal can displace a less reactive metal from a compound.
cards.explanation: Metal displacement is possible when the incoming metal is more reactive than the metal in the compound.

choices.text: A more reactive metal can displace a less reactive metal from a compound.
choices.feedback: Correct: displacement proceeds from the more reactive to the less reactive metal.

choices.text: A less reactive metal can displace a more reactive metal from a compound.
choices.feedback: This reverses the stated reactivity rule.

choices.text: Metals can displace one another regardless of their reactivity.
choices.feedback: The rule depends on one metal being more reactive.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 88-95; 5.4.1.2 The reactivity series
sources.excerpt:
A more reactive metal can displace a less reactive metal from a
compound.

cards.front: Which metals can be extracted from their oxides using carbon, and what does reduction involve?
cards.back: Metals less reactive than carbon; their oxides can be reduced with carbon, and reduction involves loss of oxygen.
cards.explanation: Carbon can reduce the oxides of metals below carbon in reactivity, and reduction is the loss of oxygen.

choices.text: Metals less reactive than carbon; their oxides can be reduced with carbon, and reduction involves loss of oxygen.
choices.feedback: Correct: both the reactivity condition and meaning of reduction are stated.

choices.text: Metals more reactive than carbon; reduction involves gain of oxygen.
choices.feedback: Both the reactivity condition and oxygen change are reversed.

choices.text: All metals; reduction leaves the amount of oxygen unchanged.
choices.feedback: The method is limited to metals less reactive than carbon, and reduction loses oxygen.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 88-95; 5.4.1.3 Extraction of metals and reduction
sources.excerpt:
Metals less reactive than carbon can be extracted from their oxides
by reduction with carbon.
Reduction involves the loss of oxygen.

cards.front: How do pH values identify neutral, acidic and alkaline aqueous solutions?
cards.back: pH 7 is neutral; acids are below 7; alkalis are above 7.
cards.explanation: A neutral solution has pH 7, while aqueous acids have lower values and aqueous alkalis have higher values.

choices.text: pH 7 is neutral; acids are below 7; alkalis are above 7.
choices.feedback: Correct: this gives the specified pH divisions.

choices.text: pH 7 is neutral; acids are above 7; alkalis are below 7.
choices.feedback: This swaps the acidic and alkaline sides of pH 7.

choices.text: pH 0 is neutral; acids and alkalis are both above 7.
choices.feedback: Neutral is pH 7, with acids below and alkalis above it.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 88-95; 5.4.2.4 The pH scale and neutralisation
sources.excerpt:
A solution with pH 7 is neutral. Aqueous solutions of acids have pH
values of less than 7 and aqueous solutions of alkalis have pH
values greater than 7.

cards.front: During electrolysis, where do positive and negative ions move, and what happens at the electrodes?
cards.back: Positive ions move to the negative cathode; negative ions move to the positive anode, where ions are discharged to produce elements.
cards.explanation: Current moves positive ions to the negative cathode and negative ions to the positive anode. Discharge at the electrodes produces elements.

choices.text: Positive ions move to the negative cathode; negative ions move to the positive anode, where ions are discharged to produce elements.
choices.feedback: Correct: each ion moves to the oppositely charged electrode.

choices.text: Positive ions move to the positive anode; negative ions move to the negative cathode.
choices.feedback: This reverses both ion directions.

choices.text: Both positive and negative ions move to the cathode and remain unchanged.
choices.feedback: The ion types move to different electrodes and are discharged.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 88-95; 5.4.3.1 The process of electrolysis
sources.excerpt:
Passing an electric current through electrolytes causes the ions to
move to the electrodes. Positively charged ions move to the
negative electrode (the cathode), and negatively charged ions move
to the positive electrode (the anode). Ions are discharged at the
electrodes producing elements. This process is called electrolysis.

cards.front: What defines an exothermic reaction in terms of energy transfer and surrounding temperature?
cards.back: It transfers energy to the surroundings, so the surroundings' temperature increases.
cards.explanation: An exothermic reaction transfers energy outward to the surroundings, causing their temperature to rise.

choices.text: It transfers energy to the surroundings, so the surroundings' temperature increases.
choices.feedback: Correct: outward energy transfer raises the surrounding temperature.

choices.text: It takes in energy from the surroundings, so the surroundings' temperature decreases.
choices.feedback: That is the stated direction and temperature change for an endothermic reaction.

choices.text: It transfers no energy, so the surroundings' temperature cannot change.
choices.feedback: An exothermic reaction transfers energy to the surroundings.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 95-97; 5.5.1.1 Energy transfer during exothermic and endothermic reactions
sources.excerpt:
An exothermic reaction is one that transfers energy to the
surroundings so the temperature of the surroundings increases.

cards.front: What defines an endothermic reaction in terms of energy transfer and surrounding temperature?
cards.back: It takes in energy from the surroundings, so the surroundings' temperature decreases.
cards.explanation: An endothermic reaction takes energy from the surroundings, causing their temperature to fall.

choices.text: It takes in energy from the surroundings, so the surroundings' temperature decreases.
choices.feedback: Correct: inward energy transfer lowers the surrounding temperature.

choices.text: It transfers energy to the surroundings, so the surroundings' temperature increases.
choices.feedback: That is the stated direction and temperature change for an exothermic reaction.

choices.text: It takes in matter rather than energy, so temperature is unaffected.
choices.feedback: The definition concerns taking in energy and a decrease in surrounding temperature.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 95-97; 5.5.1.1 Energy transfer during exothermic and endothermic reactions
sources.excerpt:
An endothermic reaction is one that takes in energy from the
surroundings so the temperature of the surroundings decreases.

cards.front: What is activation energy?
cards.back: The minimum amount of energy that particles must have to react.
cards.explanation: Activation energy is the minimum energy threshold that reacting particles must meet.

choices.text: The minimum amount of energy that particles must have to react.
choices.feedback: Correct: activation energy is a minimum energy requirement.

choices.text: The average energy possessed by all particles after a reaction.
choices.feedback: The definition is a minimum energy needed to react, not an average afterward.

choices.text: The total energy released by the products of every reaction.
choices.feedback: Activation energy concerns the energy particles must have before they can react.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 95-97; 5.5.1.2 Reaction profiles
sources.excerpt:
The minimum amount of
energy that particles must have to react is called the activation
energy.

cards.front: How can the rate of a chemical reaction be found experimentally?
cards.back: Measure the quantity of reactant used or product formed over time.
cards.explanation: Reaction rate can be determined by tracking either reactant consumption or product formation over time.

choices.text: Measure the quantity of reactant used or product formed over time.
choices.feedback: Correct: rate links a changing quantity to time.

choices.text: Measure only the final quantity of product without recording time.
choices.feedback: Time must be included when finding a reaction rate.

choices.text: Measure only the reaction temperature.
choices.feedback: The stated method measures reactant used or product formed over time.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 98-104; 5.6.1.1 Calculating rates of reactions
sources.excerpt:
The rate of a chemical reaction can be found by measuring the
quantity of a reactant used or the quantity of product formed over
time

cards.front: Using collision theory, why does increasing temperature increase reaction rate?
cards.back: Collisions become more frequent and more energetic, so the reaction rate increases.
cards.explanation: Higher temperature both increases collision frequency and makes collisions more energetic, increasing reaction rate.

choices.text: Collisions become more frequent and more energetic, so the reaction rate increases.
choices.feedback: Correct: temperature affects both collision frequency and energy.

choices.text: Collisions become less frequent and less energetic, so the reaction rate increases.
choices.feedback: Increasing temperature makes collisions more frequent and more energetic.

choices.text: The activation energy is lowered, while collision frequency stays unchanged.
choices.feedback: The stated temperature effect is on collision frequency and energy.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 98-104; 5.6.1.3 Collision theory and activation energy
sources.excerpt:
Increasing the temperature increases the frequency of collisions
and makes the collisions more energetic, and so increases the rate
of reaction.

cards.front: When is equilibrium reached in a reversible reaction?
cards.back: In apparatus that prevents reactants and products escaping, the forward and reverse reactions occur at exactly the same rate.
cards.explanation: Equilibrium requires a system that prevents escape and equal rates for the continuing forward and reverse reactions.

choices.text: In apparatus that prevents reactants and products escaping, the forward and reverse reactions occur at exactly the same rate.
choices.feedback: Correct: equilibrium is defined by equal forward and reverse rates in this apparatus.

choices.text: When equal amounts of reactants and products are present.
choices.feedback: The definition requires equal reaction rates, not equal amounts.

choices.text: When both the forward and reverse reactions have stopped.
choices.feedback: At equilibrium the two reactions occur at the same rate; they have not stopped.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 98-104; 5.6.2.3 Equilibrium
sources.excerpt:
When a reversible reaction occurs in apparatus which prevents the
escape of reactants and products, equilibrium is reached when the
forward and reverse reactions occur at exactly the same rate.

cards.front: What is the general formula for the homologous series of alkanes?
cards.back: CnH2n+2
cards.explanation: The specification gives CnH2n+2 as the general formula for the alkane homologous series.

choices.text: CnH2n+2
choices.feedback: Correct: this is the stated general formula for alkanes.

choices.text: CnH2n
choices.feedback: This is not the general alkane formula given in the specification.

choices.text: CnH2n-2
choices.feedback: The alkane formula has plus two, not minus two.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 104-107; 5.7.1.1 Crude oil, hydrocarbons and alkanes
sources.excerpt:
Most of the hydrocarbons in crude oil are hydrocarbons called
alkanes. The general formula for the homologous series of alkanes
is CnH2n+2

cards.front: What does fractional distillation separate crude oil into?
cards.back: Fractions whose molecules have a similar number of carbon atoms.
cards.explanation: Fractional distillation separates the many hydrocarbons in crude oil into fractions containing molecules with similar carbon-atom numbers.

choices.text: Fractions whose molecules have a similar number of carbon atoms.
choices.feedback: Correct: similarity in carbon-atom number characterises each fraction.

choices.text: Pure individual compounds containing identical molecules.
choices.feedback: The products are fractions containing molecules with similar carbon-atom numbers.

choices.text: Fractions whose molecules all contain the same number of hydrogen atoms.
choices.feedback: The stated similarity concerns the number of carbon atoms.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 104-107; 5.7.1.2 Fractional distillation and petrochemicals
sources.excerpt:
The many hydrocarbons in crude oil may be separated into
fractions, each of which contains molecules with a similar number of
carbon atoms, by fractional distillation.

cards.front: Which two types of hydrocarbon are included among the products of cracking?
cards.back: Alkanes and alkenes.
cards.explanation: Cracking products include alkanes and the hydrocarbon type called alkenes.

choices.text: Alkanes and alkenes.
choices.feedback: Correct: both types are included among cracking products.

choices.text: Only alkanes.
choices.feedback: Alkenes are also included among the products.

choices.text: Alkanes and carbon dioxide.
choices.feedback: The second hydrocarbon type stated is alkenes, not carbon dioxide.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 104-107; 5.7.1.4 Cracking and alkenes
sources.excerpt:
The products of cracking include alkanes and another type of
hydrocarbon called alkenes.

cards.front: In chemistry, what is a pure substance?
cards.back: A single element or compound not mixed with any other substance.
cards.explanation: Chemical purity means that the material is one element or one compound with no other substance mixed into it.

choices.text: A single element or compound not mixed with any other substance.
choices.feedback: Correct: this is the chemical definition of a pure substance.

choices.text: A single element only; compounds can never be pure.
choices.feedback: A pure substance may be either an element or a compound.

choices.text: Any natural substance that contains several unmixed-looking materials.
choices.feedback: Chemical purity requires one element or compound with nothing else mixed in.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 107-110; 5.8.1.1 Pure substances
sources.excerpt:
In chemistry, a pure substance is a single element or compound,
not mixed with any other substance.

cards.front: How is a compound's Rf value determined from a chromatogram?
cards.back: Distance moved by the compound's spot centre from the origin divided by the distance moved by the solvent.
cards.explanation: Rf is the ratio of the compound's travel distance, measured from the origin to the spot centre, to the solvent's travel distance.

choices.text: Distance moved by the compound's spot centre from the origin divided by the distance moved by the solvent.
choices.feedback: Correct: substance distance is divided by solvent distance.

choices.text: Distance moved by the solvent divided by the distance moved by the compound.
choices.feedback: This inverts the stated ratio.

choices.text: Distance moved by the solvent minus the distance moved by the compound.
choices.feedback: Rf is a ratio, not a difference between distances.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 107-110; 5.8.1.3 Chromatography
sources.excerpt:
The ratio of the distance moved by a compound (centre of spot from
origin) to the distance moved by the solvent can be expressed as its
Rf value:

cards.front: How is hydrogen tested, and what is the positive result?
cards.back: Hold a burning splint at the open end of the test tube; hydrogen burns rapidly with a pop sound.
cards.explanation: The specified hydrogen test uses a burning splint at the test tube's open end, producing a rapid burn with a pop.

choices.text: Hold a burning splint at the open end of the test tube; hydrogen burns rapidly with a pop sound.
choices.feedback: Correct: a burning splint produces the specified pop result.

choices.text: Insert a glowing splint into the test tube; the splint relights.
choices.feedback: The specified hydrogen test uses a burning splint and gives a pop.

choices.text: Bubble the gas through limewater; the limewater turns milky.
choices.feedback: The specified hydrogen result is a rapid burn with a pop sound.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 107-110; 5.8.2.1 Test for hydrogen
sources.excerpt:
The test for hydrogen uses a burning splint held at the open end of
a test tube of the gas. Hydrogen burns rapidly with a pop sound.

cards.front: What have the approximate proportions of gases in Earth's atmosphere been for the past 200 million years?
cards.back: Approximately 80% nitrogen and 20% oxygen, with small proportions of carbon dioxide, water vapour and noble gases.
cards.explanation: The atmosphere is about four-fifths nitrogen and one-fifth oxygen, with the other named gases present in small proportions.

choices.text: Approximately 80% nitrogen and 20% oxygen, with small proportions of carbon dioxide, water vapour and noble gases.
choices.feedback: Correct: these are the stated approximate proportions.

choices.text: Approximately 80% oxygen and 20% nitrogen, with no other gases.
choices.feedback: This swaps nitrogen and oxygen and omits the small proportions of other gases.

choices.text: Approximately 50% nitrogen and 50% oxygen.
choices.feedback: The stated proportions are about four-fifths nitrogen and one-fifth oxygen.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 110-115; 5.9.1.1 The proportions of different gases in the atmosphere
sources.excerpt:
For 200 million years, the proportions of different gases in the
atmosphere have been much the same as they are today:
• about four-fifths (approximately 80%) nitrogen
• about one-fifth (approximately 20%) oxygen
• small proportions of various other gases, including carbon
dioxide, water vapour and noble gases.

cards.front: According to the stated theory, what may Earth's atmosphere have been like at the start of the first billion years?
cards.back: It may have been like Mars and Venus today, consisting mainly of carbon dioxide with little or no oxygen gas.
cards.explanation: The theory cautiously suggests an early atmosphere resembling those of present-day Mars and Venus, mainly carbon dioxide with little or no oxygen.

choices.text: It may have been like Mars and Venus today, consisting mainly of carbon dioxide with little or no oxygen gas.
choices.feedback: Correct: this preserves both the proposed composition and its uncertainty.

choices.text: It definitely consisted mainly of oxygen with little or no carbon dioxide.
choices.feedback: This reverses the proposed gases and removes the source's uncertainty.

choices.text: It may have had exactly today's proportions of nitrogen and oxygen.
choices.feedback: The stated theory instead proposes mainly carbon dioxide with little or no oxygen.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 110-115; 5.9.1.2 The Earth's early atmosphere
sources.excerpt:
At the start of this period the Earth’s
atmosphere may have been like the atmospheres of Mars and
Venus today, consisting of mainly carbon dioxide with little or no
oxygen gas.

cards.front: Which greenhouse gases are named in the specification, and what role do greenhouse gases have?
cards.back: Water vapour, carbon dioxide and methane; they maintain temperatures on Earth high enough to support life.
cards.explanation: The named greenhouse gases are water vapour, carbon dioxide and methane, and atmospheric greenhouse gases maintain life-supporting temperatures.

choices.text: Water vapour, carbon dioxide and methane; they maintain temperatures on Earth high enough to support life.
choices.feedback: Correct: this gives the named gases and their stated role.

choices.text: Nitrogen, oxygen and noble gases; they maintain temperatures high enough to support life.
choices.feedback: Those are not the three greenhouse gases named in the excerpt.

choices.text: Carbon monoxide, sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides; they remove all heat from Earth.
choices.feedback: The named greenhouse gases and their stated role are different.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 110-115; 5.9.2.1 Greenhouse gases
sources.excerpt:
Greenhouse gases in the atmosphere maintain temperatures on
Earth high enough to support life. Water vapour, carbon dioxide and
methane are greenhouse gases.

cards.front: State the specified properties or effects of carbon monoxide, sulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen, and particulates.
cards.back: Carbon monoxide is toxic, colourless and odourless; sulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen cause respiratory problems and acid rain; particulates cause global dimming and health problems.
cards.explanation: Carbon monoxide is difficult to detect because it is colourless and odourless as well as toxic. Sulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen cause respiratory problems and acid rain, while particulates cause global dimming and human health problems.

choices.text: Carbon monoxide is toxic, colourless and odourless; sulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen cause respiratory problems and acid rain; particulates cause global dimming and health problems.
choices.feedback: Correct: each pollutant group is matched to its stated properties or effects.

choices.text: Carbon monoxide causes only global dimming; sulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen are harmless; particulates cause only acid rain.
choices.feedback: This mismatches all three pollutant groups with their stated effects.

choices.text: All three pollutant groups are harmless because they are easily detected.
choices.feedback: The excerpt identifies toxic, respiratory, acid-rain, global-dimming and health effects.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 110-115; 5.9.3.2 Properties and effects of atmospheric pollutants
sources.excerpt:
Carbon monoxide is a toxic gas. It is colourless and odourless and
so is not easily detected.
Sulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen cause respiratory problems in
humans and cause acid rain.
Particulates cause global dimming and health problems for humans.

cards.front: What is sustainable development?
cards.back: Development that meets current generations' needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their own needs.
cards.explanation: Sustainable development meets present needs while preserving the ability of future generations to meet theirs.

choices.text: Development that meets current generations' needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their own needs.
choices.feedback: Correct: the definition balances current and future needs.

choices.text: Development that meets current needs regardless of its effect on future generations.
choices.feedback: The ability of future generations to meet their needs must not be compromised.

choices.text: Development that reserves all resources for future generations and meets no current needs.
choices.feedback: The definition includes meeting the needs of current generations as well.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 115-120; 5.10.1.1 Using the Earth's resources and sustainable development
sources.excerpt:
Chemistry plays an important role in improving agricultural and
industrial processes to provide new products and in sustainable
development, which is development that meets the needs of current
generations without compromising the ability of future generations
to meet their own needs.

cards.front: What makes water potable, and why is potable water not chemically pure?
cards.back: It is safe to drink with sufficiently low dissolved salts and microbes, but it contains dissolved substances and is therefore not chemically pure.
cards.explanation: Potable water is safe for drinking because dissolved salts and microbes are sufficiently low. It still contains dissolved substances, so it is not pure water in the chemical sense.

choices.text: It is safe to drink with sufficiently low dissolved salts and microbes, but it contains dissolved substances and is therefore not chemically pure.
choices.feedback: Correct: potable describes safety, not chemical purity.

choices.text: It contains no dissolved substances or microbes and is chemically pure.
choices.feedback: Potable water may contain dissolved substances and is not chemically pure.

choices.text: It is any water that looks clear, regardless of dissolved salts or microbes.
choices.feedback: Drinking water must have sufficiently low levels of dissolved salts and microbes.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 115-120; 5.10.1.2 Potable water
sources.excerpt:
Water of appropriate quality is essential for life. For humans,
drinking water should have sufficiently low levels of dissolved salts
and microbes. Water that is safe to drink is called potable water.
Potable water is not pure water in the chemical sense because it
contains dissolved substances.

cards.front: What is the listed sequence of stages in sewage treatment?
cards.back: Screening and grit removal → sedimentation → anaerobic digestion of sewage sludge → aerobic biological treatment of effluent.
cards.explanation: The listed process first removes screening material and grit, then separates sludge and effluent by sedimentation, followed by anaerobic treatment of sludge and aerobic treatment of effluent.

choices.text: Screening and grit removal → sedimentation → anaerobic digestion of sewage sludge → aerobic biological treatment of effluent.
choices.feedback: Correct: this preserves the listed order and treatment streams.

choices.text: Screening and grit removal → sedimentation → aerobic digestion of sewage sludge → anaerobic treatment of effluent.
choices.feedback: This reverses the anaerobic treatment of sludge and aerobic treatment of effluent.

choices.text: Anaerobic digestion of effluent → aerobic treatment of sludge → sedimentation → screening and grit removal.
choices.feedback: This reverses the order and assigns the biological treatments to the wrong streams.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 115-120; 5.10.1.3 Waste water treatment
sources.excerpt:
Sewage treatment includes:
• screening and grit removal
• sedimentation to produce sewage sludge and effluent
• anaerobic digestion of sewage sludge
• aerobic biological treatment of effluent.

cards.front: Which stages of a product are assessed in a life cycle assessment?
cards.back: Raw-material extraction and processing; manufacturing and packaging; use and operation; disposal, including transport and distribution at each stage.
cards.explanation: An LCA assesses environmental impact from raw-material extraction through manufacture and use to end-of-life disposal, including transport and distribution throughout.

choices.text: Raw-material extraction and processing; manufacturing and packaging; use and operation; disposal, including transport and distribution at each stage.
choices.feedback: Correct: this covers the full set of listed life-cycle stages.

choices.text: Manufacturing and packaging; use and disposal only, excluding raw-material extraction.
choices.feedback: Raw-material extraction and processing must also be assessed.

choices.text: Raw-material extraction and manufacturing only, excluding use, disposal, transport and distribution.
choices.feedback: An LCA also covers use, disposal, transport and distribution.

cards.subject: Chemistry

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 115-120; 5.10.2.1 Life cycle assessment
sources.excerpt:
Life cycle assessments (LCAs) are carried out to assess the
environmental impact of products in each of these stages:
• extracting and processing raw materials
• manufacturing and packaging
• use and operation during its lifetime
• disposal at the end of its useful life, including transport and
distribution at each stage.
